title: Saga Pattern (Orchestration)
description: Central orchestrator coordinates local transactions and compensations; clearer control flow and observability.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Saga orchestration requires a central orchestrator service (VM/container: $20-200), message queue/event bus (AWS SQS/SNS: $10-500, or Kafka: $50-2000), state persistence database (RDS/DynamoDB: $30-1500), and monitoring/observability stack (CloudWatch/Datadog: $20-800). Min represents small-scale implementation with managed services on minimal instances (t3.small orchestrator, basic SQS, t3.micro RDS). Max represents production-grade deployment with HA orchestrator cluster, high-throughput message queuing, replicated state store, comprehensive distributed tracing, and multi-region capabilities. Actual costs scale with transaction volume, saga complexity, and retention requirements.
provenance.adoptionCost: High complexity implementation requiring: (1) Deep understanding of distributed transaction patterns and compensation logic (40-60 hours learning/design), (2) Building or integrating orchestrator framework (Temporal, Netflix Conductor, or custom solution: 80-120 hours), (3) Implementing saga definitions, compensation handlers, and timeout/retry logic across multiple services (60-100 hours), (4) Establishing distributed tracing and saga visualization (20-40 hours), (5) Testing complex failure scenarios and rollback paths (40-60 hours). Requires senior developers with distributed systems expertise. Total estimate: 240-380 hours at $50-80/hour blended rate, though AI assistants can reduce boilerplate code generation by 20-30%, bringing typical implementation to $4000 for medium-sized systems.
provenance.licenseCost: Open-source orchestration frameworks available: Temporal (MIT license), Netflix Conductor (Apache 2.0), Camunda (Apache 2.0 for community edition), Eclipse MicroProfile LRA. Most organizations use these OSS solutions. Commercial support/hosting options exist (Temporal Cloud: usage-based, Camunda Enterprise) but are optional. Pattern itself is architecture-agnostic and license-free. Assuming OSS stack for base implementation.

provides.reasoning: The saga orchestration pattern specifically provides coordination of distributed transactions across services through a central orchestrator that manages the execution sequence and dependencies between local transactions.

This pattern provides the ability to define and execute compensating transactions to rollback completed steps when failures occur, with the orchestrator maintaining logic to undo operations in reverse order.
The centralized orchestrator provides a single point to monitor saga state, track progress, and observe the entire transaction flow, which is a key advantage over choreography-based approaches.

The orchestration approach provides explicit centralized control over the saga flow, with the orchestrator determining the sequence and coordination of service interactions, distinguishing it from choreography patterns.
Saga orchestration IS a workflow orchestration engine — the central coordinator manages step sequencing, compensations, and transaction state across distributed services.

requires.reasoning: The pattern requires a central orchestrator component that manages saga state, coordinates service calls, handles compensation logic, and controls the execution flow of the distributed transaction.
The orchestrator needs mechanisms to communicate with participating services to invoke local transactions and compensations, whether through synchronous calls or asynchronous messaging.
The orchestrator must persist saga state to track progress, recover from failures, and ensure reliable compensation execution with exactly-once semantics.
Services participating in sagas must support idempotent operations to handle retries and ensure reliable compensation, as the orchestrator may need to retry failed operations or compensations.

supports_nfr.reason: Orchestrator as central coordination point limits availability to four nines despite distributed service architecture.
Saga orchestration adds coordination overhead including state persistence, service calls, and workflow execution steps.
At p99, orchestrator state persistence, retries, compensation logic, and network variance compound tail latency.
Orchestrator must detect failures, initiate compensations across multiple services, and restore workflow state for recovery.
Orchestrator state persistence and coordination overhead creates throughput bottleneck limiting peak to approximately 100k jobs/hour.

Saga orchestration adds significant coordination overhead (state persistence, service calls, workflow execution). At <100 jobs/hour, simpler synchronous transactions or direct service calls are more appropriate.
Saga orchestration can coordinate read operations across services up to 50k QPS before orchestrator becomes bottleneck.
Orchestrator coordination overhead and state persistence limit write throughput to approximately 10k QPS for transactional workflows.
Saga orchestration works with or without audit logging, though logging saga steps and compensations is recommended for observability.
Saga orchestration provides eventual consistency but can be configured to support read-your-writes with proper orchestrator state queries.
Saga orchestration supports both strict and relaxed durability depending on underlying service persistence configurations.
Pattern is GDPR-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is CCPA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling in the application layer, not this pattern.
Pattern is HIPAA-agnostic; compliance depends on data handling and provider BAAs, not this pattern.
Pattern is SOX-agnostic; compliance depends on audit controls in the application layer, not this pattern.

supports_constraints.reason: Saga orchestration is a cloud-agnostic architectural pattern deployable on any cloud provider or on-premises infrastructure.
Saga orchestration is an architectural pattern implementable in any programming language with workflow orchestration capabilities.
Saga orchestration is a backend coordination pattern applicable to APIs, workers, batch jobs, and data pipelines.
Saga orchestration works with or without caching layers in the coordinated services.
Saga orchestration requires async messaging to coordinate distributed transactions and trigger compensations across services.
Saga orchestration can coordinate transactions involving AI inference services or any other service type.
Saga orchestration works in both single-tenant and multi-tenant architectures.
Saga orchestration can coordinate batch processing workflows as well as real-time transactional workflows.
Saga orchestration pattern is specifically designed to manage distributed transactions across multiple services.
Saga orchestration can coordinate transactions with or without real-time streaming data sources.
Saga orchestration can coordinate transactions involving vector search services or any other data store type.
Saga orchestration works with document stores, relational databases, or any other storage type for coordinated services.
Saga orchestration works with key-value stores or any other storage type for coordinated services and orchestrator state.
Saga orchestration can coordinate transactions involving graph databases or any other data store type.
Saga orchestration can coordinate transactions involving time-series databases or any other data store type.
Saga orchestration is commonly used for OLTP workloads but also applicable to analytical and batch workflows.
Saga orchestration can coordinate OLAP workloads though it is more commonly used for transactional OLTP workflows.

orchestrator_type.description: Orchestrator architecture: centralized (single coordinator service), distributed (multiple coordinators with leader election), or hybrid (centralized with failover replicas)

compensation_strategy.description: How to handle failures: backward_recovery (rollback via compensating transactions), forward_recovery (retry until success), or manual_intervention (pause for human decision)

timeout_handling.description: Action when step times out: automatic_compensation (immediately trigger rollback), retry_then_compensate (retry before compensating), or alert_only (notify but don't auto-compensate)

state_persistence.description: Where to store saga state: database (durable ACID storage), event_store (event-sourced history), or in_memory (fast but non-durable for non-critical sagas)

retry_policy.description: Retry strategy for failed steps: exponential_backoff (increasing delays), fixed_interval (constant delay), immediate (no delay), or none (fail immediately)

max_retry_attempts.description: Maximum number of retry attempts before triggering compensation (0 means no retries)
